verses.commentary: l matters. Accept (proslambanō, προσλαμβάνω) means welcome, receive. Without quarreling over disputable matters: avoid debating opinions. The strong must welcome the weak, not judge them.

The strong eat anything (no dietary laws). The weak eat only vegetables (to avoid meat offered to idols or ceremonially unclean meat). Faith here means conviction, not saving faith. Dietary liberty is a matter of conscience, not doctrine.
The ground: God has accepted both. Divine acceptance trumps human judgment.
Each believer is accountable to their own Master (Christ). Standing or falling is determined by the Lord, not fellow believers. The Lord is able to make them stand affirms grace's sufficiency.
Some regard certain days as sacred; others treat all days alike. Each should be fully convinced (plērophoreō, πληροφορέω) in their own mind. Conviction matters more than uniformity in non-essentials.

Observing days or eating/abstaining can all be done to the Lord. Thanksgiving to God sanctifies the action. Motive, not the action itself, determines acceptability. Both practices honor God if done in faith.
Our lives belong to the Lord, not ourselves. Independence is a myth. We are part of a body, accountable to God and connected to others.
Belonging (kyriou, κυρίου) emphasizes ownership. Christ is Lord of life and death. This is comprehensive lordship.
Dead and living encompasses everyone. His resurrection qualifies Him to judge and rule. Universal lordship is the result of His redemptive work.
Why despise? Both are condemned. The reason: we all face God's judgment seat (bēma, βῆμα, tribunal). Since God judges, we must not. Our accountability is to Him, not each other.

Universal acknowledgment of God is certain. Every knee will bow (future, not hypothetical). Every tongue will acknowledge (confess). This is eschatological: all will submit to God's authority. Since God is the final Judge, human judgment is presumptuous.
Each of us (not others) will give account to God. This focuses responsibility on self, not judging others. We answer for ourselves, not for others.
Instead, focus on not causing others to stumble. Stumbling block (proskomma, πρόσκομμα) is an obstacle that trips. Obstacle (skandalon, σκάνδαλον) is a trap. Love avoids causing others to fall.

This is objective truth (echoing Mark 7:19). But subjective conscience matters: if someone regards it unclean, for them it is. Conscience binds even when mistaken. Violating conscience is sin, even in neutral matters.
Do not destroy (apollymi, ἀπόλλυμι) means do not ruin or cause to stumble. The worth of the brother: Christ died for him. Liberty must yield to love. Christ's sacrifice should constrain our freedom.
sphēmeō, βλασφημέω). Insisting on liberty can cause others to speak evil of the gospel. Guarding the gospel's reputation matters more than exercising freedom.
ng with God), peace (shalom, wholeness), and joy in the Holy Spirit. These matter infinitely more than dietary practices. Majoring on minors misses the kingdom.
matters. This pleases God and gains human approval (dokimos, δόκιμος, approved). Christlike love wins both divine and human commendation.
(oikodomē, οἰκοδομή, building up). Peace and edification are goals worth pursuing. This requires effort and intentionality.
All food is clean (affirms liberty). But eating in a way that causes stumbling is evil (kakon, κακόν). Liberty yielded for love's sake honors God.
Abstaining from meat or wine (even though permissible) to avoid causing another to fall is virtuous. Love limits liberty. Christian freedom is constrained by love for others.

Do not flaunt liberty. Blessed (makarios, μακάριος, happy) is the one who does not condemn himself. If conscience is clear (you do not violate your own conviction), you are blessed. Self-condemnation brings misery.

Eating not from faith means acting without conviction. Everything not from faith is sin. This does not mean all actions require saving faith, but all actions must align with conviction. Violating conscience is always sin, even in neutral matters.
